interactions.summary: Jiang opens by reviewing three geopolitical principles: competition inside states exceeds competition between states, elites have no loyalty, and war often preserves equilibrium rather than conquering outright.

He tests the model against the Chinese Warring States: stable great states ritualize war, Qin absorbs mercenary knowledge and lower-nobility talent, and equilibrium makes the established powers unable to imagine a barbarian takeover.
A student asks whether equilibrium means war stabilizes status competition inside countries; Jiang answers that equilibrium is a balance point where things stay the same.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer.

interactions.summary: Jiang carries the equilibrium pattern into Mesopotamia, Persia, and Greece: temple rules preserve elite status, a rule-breaker captures locked-up wealth, empires stagnate, and borderlands with energy, openness, and cohesion defeat mass, organization, and death.

Jiang turns to Greece, naming Homer, alphabet, and polis as the innovation base; contrasts Spartan land slavery and oligarchy with Athenian maritime expansion and democracy; then shows Athens, Sparta, and Thebes reaching equilibrium while Macedonia learns from all three.

He maps Alexander’s conquest of Persia onto the Qin pattern: elite overproduction and disloyal elites open the empire from inside, while Darius thinks he can bribe Alexander because equilibrium makes him underestimate unlimited conqueror appetite.

Jiang describes Alexander’s insatiable conquest, Persian elite co-optation, assassination, and the Hellenistic Age as the syncretic world where Greek, Jewish, and Persian worldviews combine toward Christianity.

He recasts Aristotle as Macedonia’s cultural imperialist: a childhood friend of Philip II who systemizes Greek knowledge for empire by replacing Plato’s spiritual ascent with material cause, telos, work, and empire-serving purpose.
Jiang returns to geography, saying Greece is poor, mountainous, maritime, and innovative because it can steal knowledge from Egypt, Anatolia, Mesopotamia, Persia, and Northern Europe.

Jiang uses maps to restate the geography of Sparta and Macedonia, then gives a harsh account of Spartan society as a Helot-control machine that deliberately separates boys from families, trains violence, and uses initiation killing.
Brief transition filler only.
He compares Greek hoplites and Persian horse archers as geography-specific innovations, explains Marathon and Thermopylae, and says Athens survives because the polis is a people, not a place.

Jiang argues Persia had already effectively won after burning Athens and only needed to exploit Sparta’s Helot weakness, but Xerxes’ desire for honor and monument leads to Salamis and the loss of the Persian navy.
A student reads Herodotus’ Persian envoy warning Athens that Xerxes’ army is too large to resist forever.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student reading quoted Herodotus passage prompted by Jiang.

interactions.summary: Jiang glosses the Persian offer as empire logic: infinite resources, repeated armies, and pressure on the small city to give up.
The Herodotus reading continues with the Athenian answer: they know Persian power is greater, but they will not make terms with Xerxes while the sun continues its course.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student reading quoted Herodotus passage, with Jiang clarification that Medes means Persians.

interactions.summary: Jiang contrasts Persian empire with Greek borderland virtue, then narrates Mardonius’ defeat at Plataea and introduces Pausanias’ refusal to mutilate the dead.
A student begins reading Pausanias’ answer rejecting the proposal to put Mardonius’ head on a pike because such behavior is barbarian, not Greek.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student reading quoted Herodotus passage, with Jiang/helping voice on pronunciation.

interactions.summary: The Pausanias passage concludes: he refuses the proposal, says Leonidas has already been avenged, and treats corpse mutilation as unworthy of Greeks.

Jiang interprets the Greek refusal as virtue before empire, then shows the Delian League becoming Athenian tribute empire and reframes the Peloponnesian War as Athens coercing allies while Sparta is pulled in to protect its Helot equilibrium.
Jiang introduces Pericles’ funeral oration, then a student reads the passage offering comfort rather than condolence and urging parents to have more children for the state.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang setup followed by student reading quoted Thucydides/Pericles passage.

interactions.summary: Jiang interprets the funeral oration as imperial logic: dead sons become useful because they died for empire, orphaned children become future soldiers, and Pericles’ equilibrium strategy creates plague in Athens.
Jiang frames the Melian Dialogue as rebellious islands trying to leave the Athenian war, then a student begins reading the Athenian argument that they will not use moral pretenses.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang setup followed by student reading Thucydides.

interactions.summary: The student continues the Melian Dialogue, including the famous claim that right is only in question among equals while the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student reading Thucydides, with Jiang glossing Lacedaemonians as Spartans.

interactions.summary: Jiang interprets Athens as worse than Persia after becoming empire, narrates the Sicilian disaster and Athens’ defeat, then returns to Philip II’s meritocratic Macedonian military package and anvil-and-hammer innovation as the tool Alexander inherits.

Jiang says Philip II had already conquered mainland Greece and prepared Persia through spies and diplomats, then explains his murder as Olympias protecting Alexander’s succession against a new Macedonian wife and son.

He presents Alexander as an Iliad-possessed sacred conqueror who imitates Achilles, seeks Zeus-Ammon’s confirmation, refuses Darius’ settlement, burns Thebes and Persepolis, and may treat destruction as ritual proof of divine sonship.

After a brief transition, Jiang argues Alexander’s killings of Philip’s loyal men were not random but a deliberate effort to create his own empire, which leads the generals to poison him and divide the Greek world.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang classroom lecture; segment 87 is a filler transition.

interactions.summary: Jiang explains the post-Alexander Greek world: few Greeks must rule many subjects, so they build cities, recruit Persian and Jewish bureaucratic help, and use Alexandria’s wealth and library to systemize Greek knowledge.

He defines the Library of Alexandria and Septuagint as cultural imperialism: Greek rule censors and promotes cultures to manage Egypt and the Levant, producing Greek-Jewish-Persian syncretism that leads to Christianity and sets up Rome next class.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is a student asking and acknowledging a clarification.
Segment contains only “All right” and has no substantive speaker content.
Segment is quoted material read aloud by a student after Jiang says “Can you read please?”.
These are quoted Herodotus passages read aloud, not Jiang’s own prose.
Short pronunciation repair for “Agenitai” occurs inside the reading.
Conclusion of quoted Herodotus passage read aloud by a student.
Quoted funeral oration passage read aloud by a student; segment 67 is a pronunciation correction.
Beginning of quoted Melian Dialogue read aloud by a student.
Quoted Melian Dialogue read aloud; Jiang briefly clarifies “Lacedaemonians” means Spartans.
speaker_notes.suggested_speaker: student reading Thucydides with Jiang aside

speaker_notes.note: Segment 87 is labeled UNKNOWN but likely a brief transition or room sound; no substantive speaker uncertainty affects the packet.

claims.claim: Jiang defines elite overproduction as status competition inside the state, often between upper and lower nobility rather than simply rich and poor.
He argues that elites have no real loyalty to people, state, or family; they do whatever accumulates power.
He frames war as a mechanism for maintaining equilibrium and status quo, using World War I and the Warring States as examples.
He says Qin conquered because stable states became stagnant while Qin received mercenary knowledge, innovation, low-nobility talent, and social energy.
Equilibrium means a balance point where things stay the same and participants assume they will stay the same forever.
Jiang interprets Mesopotamian temple immunity as an equilibrium rule: war is allowed, but temple wealth cannot be touched, so expansion is constrained.
He says the Greek-Persian encounter follows the borderland pattern: Greeks learn Persian tactics as mercenaries, gain wealth through piracy, provoke invasion, and then defeat a much larger empire.
He defines empire advantages as mass, organization, and death, while borderlands counter with energy, openness, and cohesion.
Jiang credits Greek innovation to Homer, alphabetic writing, and the polis.
He explains Sparta as a conservative oligarchic land society built around controlling Helot labor, while Athens becomes a democratic naval society because rowers fight and therefore vote.
Macedonia arises when Greek city-states reach equilibrium; Philip II combines the military innovations of Sparta, Thebes, and Athens and recruits their talent.
Alexander’s Persian conquest is explained through elite disloyalty and equilibrium blindness, not merely battlefield superiority.
Jiang says the Hellenistic Age is defined by syncretism among Jews, Greeks, and Persians, producing the basis for Western civilization and Christianity.

He claims Philip II and Aristotle were childhood best friends and reads that as evidence of a long Macedonian plan: Philip absorbs Greek military innovation while Aristotle absorbs Greek intellectual innovation.
He frames Aristotle’s philosophy as empire knowledge: material cause-and-effect plus telos makes individuals work hard and produce energy for empire.
He contrasts Plato as rationalist/mind-over-matter with Aristotle as empiricist/matter-focused, then says empires including British and American empire adopt Aristotle’s orientation.
Macedonia is initially poor, backward, mountainous, and often subject to Persia, so its later conquest repeats the marginal-power pattern.
Spartan society is organized around keeping Helots under control; Helots are both Sparta’s resource and its danger.
Greek hoplites and Persian horse archers develop from different geographies; Persia cannot use its horse-archer advantage effectively in Greece.
Athens survives the burning of the city by treating the polis as the people rather than the physical place.
Jiang says Xerxes could have won by avoiding naval confrontation and exploiting Sparta’s Helot weakness, but imperial arrogance made him seek Salamis.
He treats Herodotus as valuable but memory-based and therefore something to take with a grain of salt.
Themistocles and the Athenian trireme exploit Greek naval design and head-on Persian mistakes to scatter the Persian navy.
The Persian envoy passage embodies empire’s advantage of mass, organization, and death: inexhaustible resources against a small city.
claims.temporal_scope: Quoted source interpreted in lecture on 2025-11-20.

claims.claim: The Athenian reply to Persia presents borderland virtue: liberty, gods, heroes, and refusal to make terms with a barbarian despite Persian superiority.
claims.temporal_scope: Quoted source interpreted in lecture on 2025-11-20.

claims.claim: Jiang says Greek victory still carried an ethic of virtue, faith, loyalty to gods, and refusal to behave like barbarians.
Pausanias’ refusal to mutilate Mardonius shows Jiang’s early-Greek contrast between Greek virtue and barbarian violence.
claims.temporal_scope: Quoted source interpreted in lecture on 2025-11-20.

claims.claim: Jiang says the Delian League begins as an anti-Persian defense fund but becomes Athenian empire when Athens takes the treasury and demands tribute.
He rejects the familiar Thucydides-trap frame by saying Athens, not Sparta, was the hegemonic empire and its allies dragged Sparta into a war Athens made unavoidable.
Athens and Sparta avoid decisive options because each would destroy its own social equilibrium by freeing Helots or letting them control Sparta.
He interprets Pericles’ funeral oration as a speech about empire rather than democracy.
Pericles’ rhetoric converts dead sons and orphaned children into resources for imperial reproduction.
Pericles’ plague strategy results from trying to preserve balance rather than decisively invading Sparta.
Jiang introduces the Melian Dialogue as evidence that Athens coerced allies and islands that wanted out of its war.
Jiang reads the Melian Dialogue as Athens abandoning liberty rhetoric for pure imperial might: the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must.
claims.temporal_scope: Quoted source interpreted in lecture on 2025-11-20.

claims.claim: Athens’ Sicilian expedition is an empire’s lazy, stupid, arrogant mistake that brings Syracuse, Sparta, Persia, and allies against it and ends the Peloponnesian War.
Philip II creates the Macedonian war machine by combining Persian, Athenian, Spartan, and Theban innovations with meritocracy and morale.
The anvil-and-hammer tactic locks the enemy with infantry and smashes it with cavalry, enabling Alexander’s conquest of Persia.
Jiang says Philip II was ready to invade Persia in 336 after conquering mainland Greece and preparing diplomats and spies inside Persia.
He attributes Philip II’s murder to Olympias protecting Alexander’s claim after Philip produced a Macedonian son who threatened Alexander’s succession.
Alexander’s self-conception as son of God explains his refusal to stop at Darius’ offer, his drive toward India, and possibly ritualized destruction at Thebes and Persepolis.
Alexander killed Philip’s loyal men to make his own empire, which caused his generals to fear they were next and conspire to poison him.
After Alexander, Greek rulers face a manpower problem and govern by founding Greek cities, settling veterans, inviting Greeks, and recruiting Persian bureaucrats and Jews.
Jiang defines cultural imperialism as creating a cultural understanding of the world that allows empire to rule over people.
He says the Library of Alexandria systemizes Greek knowledge into textbooks and functions as a university-like tool of empire, not merely education.
He reads the Septuagint as a Ptolemaic strategy to bribe Jewish priests, control the Levant, and fold Jewish culture into Greek imperial administration.
He says Greek, Jewish, and Persian worldviews merge in the Hellenistic empire and give rise to Christianity, which Rome will later amplify.

signature_moments.moment: The world is Game of Thrones: power lovers will kill parents, brothers, and children to accumulate power.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the world is Game of Thrones
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the elite-loyalty principle its brutal emotional shape.

signature_moments.moment: Stable is just another word for stagnant, and equilibrium makes its insiders lazy, stupid, and arrogant.
signature_moments.source_phrase: stable is stagnant
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This phrase drives the whole lecture: equilibrium creates the blindness that lets the margin win.

signature_moments.moment: Temple wealth is protected by divine fear until a rule-breaker discovers the gods do not strike him down.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Screw this system. I'm going to go for it all
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The Lugazagi story dramatizes how an equilibrium collapses when someone violates the sacred rule keeping wealth locked up.

signature_moments.moment: Empire has mass, organization, and death; the borderland answers with energy, openness, and cohesion.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Mass, organization, and death... energy, openness, and cohesion
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This paired formula becomes the lecture’s portable model for Qin, Greece, Macedonia, and later Rome.

signature_moments.moment: Greek civilization is made from Homer, the alphabet, and the polis: myth, transmission, and citizen politics in one package.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Homer... the alphabet... the polis
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives Greece its creative engine before the lecture turns it into empire.

signature_moments.moment: A navy forces democracy because anyone can row.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if you're a navy, you have to be a democracy
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The political form is derived from military technology and participation, not abstract ideals.

signature_moments.moment: Darius offers territory and treasury, but Alexander wants all of it because he thinks he is the son of God.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I want it all
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The equilibrium bargain fails against an unlimited sacred conqueror.

signature_moments.moment: Christianity is born from the Greek world as syncretism: Jews, Greeks, and Persians forced into one imperial mixture.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a combining of religions... called Christianity
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The lecture makes Christianity a geopolitical-cultural product of Hellenistic rule, not a free-floating doctrine.

signature_moments.moment: Philip conquers with armies while Aristotle conquers with systemized knowledge.
signature_moments.source_phrase: systemize it into a knowledge of empire
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns philosophy into an imperial technology.

signature_moments.moment: Telos becomes the empire’s moral engine: fulfill your purpose, work hard, and produce energy for the empire.
signature_moments.source_phrase: work hard and produce energy for the empire
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The abstract philosophical term is made into a labor discipline.

signature_moments.moment: Sparta manufactures warriors by manufacturing anxiety: remove boys from parents, beat them daily, bind them to mentors, then graduate them through killing.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you have to create anxiety in them
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves Jiang’s psychological reading of military societies.

signature_moments.moment: A polis is not a place. It is a people who can sail away and keep the war alive.
signature_moments.source_phrase: A polis is not a place it's a people
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the Greek civic reversal that keeps Athens from being destroyed when Athens burns.

signature_moments.moment: The empire has already won, but it risks everything because Xerxes wants a monument to himself.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I want to go back and build a monument to myself
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes imperial arrogance the causal mistake that keeps Greece alive.

signature_moments.moment: Empire speaks in endless armies: we are infinite in wealth; we can throw army after army at you.
signature_moments.source_phrase: army after army after army
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang’s empire triad dramatized in a historical threat.

signature_moments.moment: The small city answers infinite empire with liberty, gods, heroes, and refusal.
signature_moments.source_phrase: so long as the sun shall continue
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang’s cleanest contrast between borderland cohesion and imperial mass.

signature_moments.moment: The defense alliance becomes a protection racket: the gold moves to Athens and tribute replaces Persia.
signature_moments.source_phrase: it's no longer a alliance but a empire
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the hinge from Athens-the-liberator to Athens-the-mafia-state.

signature_moments.moment: Both sides know how to win, but winning would destroy the social order each side actually wants to preserve.
signature_moments.source_phrase: maintain the equilibrium
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns the Peloponnesian War into equilibrium theater rather than strategic confusion.

signature_moments.moment: Pericles tells grieving parents the empire can redeem useless sons by turning their deaths and orphans into more soldiers.
signature_moments.source_phrase: this is now empire guys
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture’s darkest contrast between democratic rhetoric and imperial reproduction.

signature_moments.moment: Put everyone inside the walls to preserve strategy, and the city manufactures its own plague.
signature_moments.source_phrase: guess what happens if you put a lot of people in one place
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The equilibrium-preserving tactic becomes self-destruction.

signature_moments.moment: Athens moves from liberty to “shut up and obey”: the weak suffer what they must.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the moral collapse of the former borderland into empire.

signature_moments.moment: Philip’s army is an anvil and hammer: infantry pins you in place, cavalry smashes you apart.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the anvil and the hammer
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives Macedonia’s military synthesis a memorable physical image.

signature_moments.moment: Alexander carries the Iliad, reenacts Achilles, and walks to Ammon to hear that he is not Philip’s son but God’s.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you are not the son of Philip II you're the son of God
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The lecture makes myth into Alexander’s operating system.

signature_moments.moment: The atrocities may be ritual sacrifices: burning cities to prove he is the son of God.
signature_moments.source_phrase: ritual sacrifices necessary to prove that he was the son of God
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves Jiang’s provocative move from cruelty to sacred-political performance.

signature_moments.moment: Alexander’s “random” violence becomes statecraft: killing the father’s men to create his own empire.
signature_moments.source_phrase: random acts of violence but they're not
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns biography into imperial consolidation logic.

signature_moments.moment: A university is not really education; it is an empire’s machine for producing a worldview that lets it rule.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a university is not really about education... it's really about a tool of empire
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the episode its institutional sting and links Alexandria to later imperial knowledge systems.

signature_moments.moment: The Greeks bribe the Jews by celebrating their Bible in Greek, turning respect for tradition into imperial control.
signature_moments.source_phrase: let us translate your bible into Greek
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes cultural inclusion part of domination rather than simple tolerance.

signature_moments.moment: Christianity appears as merged worldview: Greeks ruling, Persians administering, Jews helping build the empire.
signature_moments.source_phrase: this will merge worldviews and this will give us Christianity
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the final causal chain from Hellenistic empire to Rome and Christianity.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Too many elites competing for limited positions of status and power, producing internal crisis.
A status-quo balance that stabilizes elites but also creates stagnation and blindness.
Innovation, ideas, talent, and motivational force flowing into a poorer borderland power.
Poor or marginal peoples labeled barbarians who can defeat empires through energy, openness, and cohesion.
Jiang’s triad for empire: population scale, bureaucracy, and willingness to spend lives repeatedly.
Jiang’s counter-triad for borderlands: hard work, learning from others, and social unity.
Greek citizen political unit where citizens participate in public life; often small but numerous.
Conquered slave/serf population whose labor supports Sparta and whose rebellion risk keeps Sparta conservative.
Poor, backward, formerly marginal power that unites Greece by absorbing Greek military and talent innovations.
Combining religions or worldviews, here Greek, Jewish, and Persian, into the basis of Western civilization and Christianity.
Purpose or end; in Jiang’s reading, Aristotle’s way of directing individuals toward productive roles.
Systemized knowledge that helps empire organize labor, culture, and purpose.
Greek heavy infantry formation suited to hilly Greek geography and decisive in infantry combat.
Persian military innovation suited to wide terrain but neutralized in Greece.
Jiang’s decisive naval battle where Persian arrogance risks the navy and changes world history.
Historian of the Persian Wars, useful and entertaining but based on collected memories after the fact.
In the quoted passage, Jiang clarifies Medes as Persians.
Greek self-understanding as loyalty to gods, city, liberty, and right conduct against imperial power.
Anti-Persian alliance fund that Jiang says Athens converts into empire and tribute.
Jiang’s label for Athenian empire as tribute extraction and coercion over allies.
Pericles’ famous speech, interpreted by Jiang as imperial rather than democratic rhetoric.
Thucydides passage Jiang uses to show Athenian empire speaking openly in coercive terms.
Macedonian tactic where infantry fixes the enemy and cavalry strikes decisively.
Philip II’s morale system of promoting good soldiers and demoting bad ones, even when it overturns aristocratic hierarchy.
Syncretic god/oracle Jiang says confirms Alexander’s divine sonship and world-conquest destiny.
Jiang’s implicit type for conquerors whose appetite is justified by divine sonship and world-unification destiny.
Imperial promotion, systemization, and censorship of culture to justify rule and manage subject peoples.
Hellenistic institution Jiang treats as a university-like empire tool for systemizing Greek knowledge.
Greek translation of the Jewish Bible, interpreted by Jiang as a Ptolemaic project to recruit Jewish elites and control the Levant.
Institutional complex associated with the Library of Alexandria, part of the empire’s knowledge apparatus.

chronology_notes.note: This 2025-11-20 lecture begins Secret History #20 by restating the cross-civilizational equilibrium model before applying it to Greece.
The lecture presents Mesopotamia, Persia, and Greece as iterations of the same equilibrium-to-borderland conquest model.
Jiang flags Rome as the next power after the Hellenistic equilibrium; this lecture stops before the Rome class.
The lecture positions Aristotle and Hellenistic syncretism after Alexander’s conquest and before the later Roman/Christian synthesis.
The lecture marks Marathon in 490 BCE and then the later Persian invasion through Thermopylae and Athens.
This packet narrates the Persian Wars from Thermopylae and Athens’ burning into Salamis and Xerxes’ attempted settlement.
The packet continues the Persian War source readings from Athenian defiance into the setup for Pausanias’ moral refusal.
The packet marks the transition from Persian War virtue to postwar Athenian empire and the Peloponnesian War equilibrium.
The packet moves from Pericles’ wartime rhetoric and plague to the Melian Dialogue as Athens’ imperial language becomes explicit.
The packet moves from Athenian imperial defeat back to the Macedonian synthesis that succeeds the Greek city-state equilibrium.
The packet narrates 336 BCE succession, Alexander’s Persian conquest, divine claims, and death before the Hellenistic division.
The lecture closes by linking the Hellenistic world to Christianity and previewing Rome as the next class after 2025-11-20.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders Zhao/Wei/Chu/Qin inconsistently, but the Warring States analogy is clear from context.
ASR names Lugazagi/Umma/Sargon/Zagros imperfectly, but the sequence of temple-rule violation, Akkadian empire, and Persian borderland is intelligible.
The assassination claim is stated strongly by Jiang; later packets refine the poisoning/conspiracy story.
ASR likely renders the Spartan initiation age phrase as “1819”; context indicates ages eighteen or nineteen.
ASR renders Themistocles as “Femescicles”; context clearly points to Themistocles.
The quoted Herodotus reading contains ASR and pronunciation errors, but the moral point of refusing corpse mutilation is clear.
Quoted-source readings contain ASR errors, but Jiang’s interpretation around them identifies the passages.
Jiang presents contested assassination/poisoning claims assertively; confidence is medium because the lecture itself notes debate around Philip’s murder.
ASR garbles Ptolemy/Soter naming and Greek playwright names, but the Library of Alexandria anecdote is clear.
